title: Find Duplicate Number
description: Given an array containing n+1 integers where each integer is between 1 and n find the duplicate number

examples.explanation: Array length = 5. i=0 (value=1): j=1 (3) no match, j=2 (4) no match, j=3 (2) no match, j=4 (2) no match. i=1 (value=3): j=2 (4) no match, j=3 (2) no match, j=4 (2) no match. i=2 (value=4): j=3 (2) no match, j=4 (2) no match. i=3 (value=2): j=4 (2) MATCH found! Return 2.
Array length = 5. i=0 (value=3): j=1 (1) no match, j=2 (3) MATCH found! Return 3. (Algorithm stops here, doesn't check remaining pairs)

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare each element with every other element in the array to find a duplicate. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution comparing each element with every other element to find duplicate
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the duplicate number problem. The intuition is straightforward: if we have an array where each number should be unique between 1 and n, but we have n+1 elements, there must be exactly one duplicate. The brute force approach simply checks every possible pair of elements to find which two positions contain the same value. We use nested loops: the outer loop selects each element as a candidate, and the inner loop compares it against all subsequent elements. When we find two elements with the same value, we've found our duplicate. This approach works because it's exhaustive - it checks every possible pair combination. The time complexity is O(n²) because for n+1 elements, we perform approximately (n+1)²/2 comparisons. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the inner comparison loop. Common mistakes include: 1) Comparing an element with itself (need j > i), 2) Not handling edge cases like single element arrays (though constraints prevent this), 3) Returning too early (should return only when duplicate found). The algorithm: 1) Get array length n_plus_one, 2) Outer loop i from 0 to n_plus_one-2, 3) Inner loop j from i+1 to n_plus_one-1, 4) If arr[i] == arr[j], return arr[i], 5) If loops complete without finding, return -1 (shouldn't happen per problem constraints).

examples.trace: Array length = 5. i=0 (value=1): j=1 (3) no match, j=2 (4) no match, j=3 (2) no match, j=4 (2) no match. i=1 (value=3): j=2 (4) no match, j=3 (2) no match, j=4 (2) no match. i=2 (value=4): j=3 (2) no match, j=4 (2) no match. i=3 (value=2): j=4 (2) MATCH found! Return 2.
Array length = 5. i=0 (value=3): j=1 (1) no match, j=2 (3) MATCH found! Return 3. (Algorithm stops here, doesn't check remaining pairs)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pairwise Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: The nested loop structure that compares each element with all subsequent elements
ladders.explanation: This is the core algorithmic structure of the brute force approach: comparing each element with every element that comes after it. The intuition is that to find duplicates without extra memory, we need to systematically check all possible pairs. We avoid comparing elements with themselves or re-checking pairs by having the inner loop start at i+1. This ensures each pair is checked exactly once. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Receive array arr of length n, 2) For i from 0 to n-2 (outer loop), 3) For j from i+1 to n-1 (inner loop), 4) If arr[i] == arr[j], return true (or the value), 5) If loops complete, return false. Time complexity is O(n²) because the inner loop runs approximately n(n-1)/2 times. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 as the complete implementation, and to L2 which is the equality comparison operation. Common mistakes: 1) Starting inner loop at 0 instead of i+1 (wastes time comparing already-checked pairs), 2) Using <= instead of < in loop conditions (causes index out of bounds), 3) Not considering that n here is actually n+1 from the original problem. The real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)-1): for j in range(i+1, len(arr)): if arr[i] == arr[j]: return arr[i].

examples.trace: Array length = 4. i=0 (value=1): j=1 (2) no match, j=2 (3) no match, j=3 (2) no match. i=1 (value=2): j=2 (3) no match, j=3 (2) MATCH found! Return 2.

Array length = 5. i=0 (value=4): j=1 (5) no match, j=2 (6) no match, j=3 (7) no match, j=4 (8) no match. i=1 (value=5): j=2 (6) no match, j=3 (7) no match, j=4 (8) no match. i=2 (value=6): j=3 (7) no match, j=4 (8) no match. i=3 (value=7): j=4 (8) no match. All comparisons complete, no duplicate found.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Equality Check in Array
ladders.desc: Checking if two array elements have the same value
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental comparison operation at the heart of duplicate detection: determining whether two elements in an array contain identical values. The intuition is simple - if two different positions in the array store the same value, we've found a duplicate. This operation doesn't care about the positions themselves, only whether the values match. The algorithm: 1) Access element at index i, 2) Access element at index j, 3) Compare the two values using equality operator (==), 4) Return true if equal, false otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the operation performed inside the nested loops, and to L3 which is array indexing. Common mistakes: 1) Comparing indices instead of values (i == j instead of arr[i] == arr[j]), 2) Using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), 3) Not verifying indices are within bounds first. The real code pattern: if arr[i] == arr[j]: return True else: return False. This operation is deceptively simple but crucial - it's what makes the brute force approach work. Every duplicate detection algorithm ultimately reduces to some form of value comparison.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[0] = 5. Step 2: Access arr[2] = 7. Step 3: Compare 5 == 7. Step 4: 5 does not equal 7, so return false.
Step 1: Access arr[0] = 4. Step 2: Access arr[2] = 4. Step 3: Compare 4 == 4. Step 4: 4 equals 4, so return true.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Traversal
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements by index and iterating through arrays
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on how to access individual elements in an array and systematically move through all elements. Arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where each element can be accessed in O(1) time using its index (position). The intuition is that arrays provide random access - we can jump directly to any position if we know its index. For duplicate detection, we need to access elements at specific positions to compare them. The algorithm for traversing an array: 1) Determine array length n, 2) Use a loop variable i that goes from 0 to n-1, 3) In each iteration, access arr[i] to get the current element. Time complexity for full traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as how we get the elements to compare, and to L4 which is the basic loop construct. Common mistakes: 1) Using 1-based indexing (arrays typically start at index 0), 2) Going beyond n-1 (causing index out of bounds), 3) Confusing the index with the value stored at that index. The real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): current_element = arr[i]. Array traversal is fundamental to almost all array algorithms. In the brute force approach, we use two indices (i and j) to access different elements for comparison.

examples.trace: Array length = 3. i=0: access arr[0] = 10. i=1: access arr[1] = 20. i=2: access arr[2] = 30. End of array reached.
Step 1: Access arr[0] = 5. Step 2: Access arr[2] = 7. Step 3: Access arr[3] = 1. Each access is independent and takes O(1) time.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Loop Control Structures
ladders.desc: Using for/while loops to repeat operations
ladders.explanation: This level covers loop constructs, which allow us to execute the same code multiple times - essential for checking all elements in an array. The intuition is that instead of writing separate code for each array position, we use a loop to systematically apply the same logic to every element. A for loop typically has: initialization (starting value), condition (when to continue), and increment (how to move to next iteration). For array traversal, we usually loop from 0 to length-1. The algorithm: 1) Initialize counter i = 0, 2) While i < array_length, 3) Execute loop body (e.g., access arr[i]), 4) Increment i by 1, 5) Repeat until condition fails. Time complexity depends on loop body. Space complexity is O(1) for the counter variable. This connects to L3 as how we implement array traversal, and to L5 which is basic conditional logic. Common mistakes: 1) Infinite loops (forgetting to increment), 2) Off-by-one errors (using <= instead of <), 3) Modifying the loop counter inside the body (usually problematic). The real code pattern: for i in range(n): # do something with i. In the brute force solution, we use nested loops: an outer loop for the first element and an inner loop for all subsequent elements.

examples.trace: Initialize i=0. Check i<5: true, print 0. i=1: check true, print 1. i=2: check true, print 2. i=3: check true, print 3. i=4: check true, print 4. i=5: check false, loop ends.
Initialize sum=0, i=1. Check i<=3: true, sum=0+1=1, i=2. Check true, sum=1+2=3, i=3. Check true, sum=3+3=6, i=4. Check false, loop ends. Return sum=6.

testCases.input: Loop i=0 to 4 step 2, print i

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variables and Comparisons
ladders.desc: Storing values in variables and comparing them
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental level: understanding variables (named storage locations for data) and comparison operations (checking relationships between values). The intuition is that to find duplicates, we need to store values temporarily and check if they're equal. A variable has a name, a type, and a value. Comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, etc.) return true or false based on the relationship between two values. The algorithm for comparing two numbers: 1) Store first value in variable a, 2) Store second value in variable b, 3) Apply comparison operator: a == b, 4) Get result (true if equal, false otherwise). Time and space complexity are both O(1). This connects to L4 as what we do inside loops, and forms the foundation for all higher levels. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing = (assignment) with == (comparison), 2) Not initializing variables before use, 3) Comparing incompatible types. The real code pattern: a = 5; b = 5; result = (a == b) # result is True. This simple concept scales up: in the brute force solution, arr[i] and arr[j] are variables holding array elements, and we compare them with ==.

examples.trace: Step 1: Let a = 7. Step 2: Let b = 7. Step 3: Evaluate a == b. Step 4: 7 equals 7, so result is true.
Step 1: Let a = 4. Step 2: Let b = 9. Step 3: Evaluate a == b. Step 4: 4 does not equal 9, so result is false.

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash set to track seen numbers while iterating through the array; the first duplicate found is returned. This approach is efficient in time but uses extra space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Duplicate Number Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a hash set to find the duplicate number in an array of n+1 integers between 1 and n.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is straightforward: since we need to identify a duplicate element in the array, we can track which numbers we've already encountered. A hash set provides O(1) average-time operations for insertion and lookup, making it ideal for this purpose. As we iterate through the array, we check if the current number is already in the set. If it is, we've found the duplicate and return it immediately. If not, we add it to the set and continue. This works because the problem guarantees exactly one duplicate number (though it may appear more than twice), and we're looking for any duplicate occurrence. The algorithm is: 1. Initialize an empty hash set. 2. For each element in the array: a. If the element is in the set, return it as the duplicate. b. Otherwise, add the element to the set. 3. Since the problem guarantees a duplicate, we'll always return in step 2a. Time complexity is O(n) because we perform constant-time operations for each of the n+1 elements. Space complexity is O(n) because in the worst case (no duplicate until the end), we store all n unique elements in the set. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of checking and updating the hash set, which is detailed in L1. Common mistakes include: not handling edge cases like empty arrays (though constraints prevent this), using a list instead of a set for lookups (which would be O(n) per check), or continuing to search after finding the duplicate (wasting time). Real code pattern: def findDuplicate(nums): seen = set(); for num in nums: if num in seen: return num; seen.add(num).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize seen = {}. Step 2: Process nums[0]=1, not in seen, add: seen={1}. Step 3: Process nums[1]=3, not in seen, add: seen={1,3}. Step 4: Process nums[2]=4, not in seen, add: seen={1,3,4}. Step 5: Process nums[3]=2, not in seen, add: seen={1,3,4,2}. Step 6: Process nums[4]=2, in seen (2 is in set), return 2.

Step 1: Initialize seen = {}. Step 2: Process nums[0]=3, not in seen, add: seen={3}. Step 3: Process nums[1]=1, not in seen, add: seen={3,1}. Step 4: Process nums[2]=3, in seen (3 is in set), return 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check and Update Hash Set for Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that checks if a number is in a hash set and updates it.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the hash set approach. The intuition is to maintain a set of seen numbers and, for each new number, determine if it has been seen before. If it has, we've identified a duplicate; if not, we record it for future checks. This works because sets provide average O(1) membership testing and insertion, allowing efficient duplicate detection in linear time. The algorithm: 1. Input: an array of integers and an initially empty hash set. 2. For each number in the array: a. Check if the number is in the set using the 'in' operator. b. If yes, return the number (duplicate found). c. If no, add the number to the set using 'add'. 3. Since the problem guarantees a duplicate, we'll always return in step 2b. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, with each step taking O(1) on average. Space complexity is O(n) for storing up to n elements in the set. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is directly used in the full solution by iterating over the entire array. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of using hash sets for membership testing. Common mistakes: forgetting to initialize the set, using a list for lookups (O(n) time), or not returning immediately upon finding a duplicate. Real code pattern: def checkDuplicate(nums): seen = set(); for num in nums: if num in seen: return num; seen.add(num).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with seen={}. Step 2: Process 1, not in seen, add: seen={1}. Step 3: Process 2, not in seen, add: seen={1,2}. Step 4: Process 3, not in seen, add: seen={1,2,3}. Step 5: Process 2, in seen (2 is in set), return 2.

Step 1: Start with seen={}. Step 2: Process 4, not in seen, add: seen={4}. Step 3: Process 5, not in seen, add: seen={4,5}. Step 4: Process 6, not in seen, add: seen={4,5,6}. Step 5: Process 4, in seen (4 is in set), return 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Hash Sets for Membership Testing
ladders.desc: Understand how hash sets enable O(1) average-time checks for element existence.
ladders.explanation: The core logic here is leveraging hash sets for efficient membership testing. The intuition is that a hash set stores elements in a way that allows quick lookup by using a hash function to map elements to indices in an underlying array. When we check if an element is in the set, the hash function computes its potential index, and we can verify presence in O(1) average time, assuming a good hash function and load factor. This is crucial for the duplicate detection problem because we need to test each element against previously seen elements rapidly. The algorithm for membership testing: 1. Compute the hash of the element. 2. Use this hash to index into the set's internal storage. 3. Check if the element exists at that location (handling collisions with techniques like chaining or open addressing). 4. Return True if found, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(1) on average for lookup and insertion, but O(n) in worst case due to collisions. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. Connection to L1: This logic is applied in the check-and-update subroutine by using 'in' and 'add' operations. Connection to L3: It builds on the concept of hash functions. Common mistakes: assuming worst-case O(1) time (it's average-case), not considering collision handling, or confusing sets with lists (which have O(n) lookup). Real code pattern: In Python, 'num in seen' uses hash set logic internally; we don't implement it manually, but understand it uses hashing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Hash function computes hash(2)=h. Step 2: Use h to index into set's internal array. Step 3: At index h, find element 2 (or handle collision if needed). Step 4: Return True since 2 is present.
Step 1: Hash function computes hash(7)=h. Step 2: Use h to index into set's internal array. Step 3: At index h, no element 7 found (or empty slot). Step 4: Return False since 7 is not present.

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Functions and Collision Handling
ladders.desc: Learn how hash functions map elements to indices and manage collisions in data structures.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers hash functions and collision handling, which are essential for implementing efficient hash sets. The intuition is that a hash function takes an input (like an integer) and returns a fixed-size value (hash code) used as an index in an array. For duplicate detection, we hash integers to quickly locate them in a set. However, different inputs might produce the same hash (collision), so we need strategies to handle this. Common methods include chaining (storing a list of elements at each index) or open addressing (probing for the next empty slot). The algorithm for hashing an integer: 1. Take the integer value. 2. Apply a hash function (e.g., modulo a prime number) to compute an index. 3. Use this index to access the storage array. For collision handling: if the slot is occupied, use chaining to add to a list or open addressing to find another slot. Time complexity: hashing is O(1), but collision resolution can add overhead, though average-case remains O(1) with good design. Space complexity depends on the storage array size and collision lists. Connection to L2: Hash functions enable the O(1) membership testing in sets. Connection to L4: It relies on basic array operations for storage. Common mistakes: using a poor hash function (e.g., identity for integers might work but modulo is common), ignoring collisions, or not resizing the array to maintain load factor. Real code pattern: In practice, languages like Python handle this internally, but for learning: def hash_func(num, size): return num % size; then store in array at that index, with collision lists if needed.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute hash: h(7) = 7 % 5 = 2. Step 2: Use index 2 in array. If collision occurs (e.g., if index 2 already has an element), handle by chaining (add to list) or open addressing (find next empty slot).
Step 1: Compute hash: h(12) = 12 % 5 = 2. Step 2: Index 2 same as for 7, so collision occurs. For chaining: add 12 to list at index 2. For open addressing: probe to index 3 if empty.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Iteration and Element Access
ladders.desc: Master iterating through an array and accessing its elements sequentially.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing an array from start to end and accessing each element. The intuition is that to process all elements in a collection (like checking for duplicates), we need to visit each one exactly once. In programming, this is typically done with a loop (e.g., for or while) that goes through indices or directly through elements. For the duplicate problem, we iterate through the array to examine each integer. The algorithm: 1. Initialize an index or iterator. 2. While there are more elements: a. Access the current element using its index or iterator. b. Perform an operation (e.g., check against a set). c. Move to the next element. 3. Stop when all elements are processed. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, as each access is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) extra space for the iteration variables. Connection to L3: Iteration is used to apply hash functions to each element. Connection to L5: It builds on the concept of arrays as indexed collections. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors (e.g., accessing beyond array bounds), using the wrong loop type, or modifying the array during iteration (which can cause issues). Real code pattern: In Python: for num in nums: # access num directly; or for i in range(len(nums)): num = nums[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at index 0, access nums[0]=1, print 1. Step 2: Move to index 1, access nums[1]=2, print 2. Step 3: Move to index 2, access nums[2]=3, print 3. Step 4: End as no more indices.
Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: Index 0, access 4, sum=0+4=4. Step 3: Index 1, access 5, sum=4+5=9. Step 4: Index 2, access 6, sum=9+6=15. Step 5: Return sum=15.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Data Types
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as collections of elements and basic integer operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers arrays and basic data types, which are prerequisites for the duplicate number problem. An array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size sequence of elements of the same type, accessible by index. For this problem, we use an array of integers. The intuition is that arrays allow us to group multiple numbers together so we can process them collectively. Basic integer operations include assignment, comparison, and arithmetic, which are used to manipulate and check values. In the context of the problem, we have an array where each element is an integer between 1 and n, and we need to compare these integers to find duplicates. The algorithm at this level is simple: define an array, access its elements, and perform comparisons. For example, to check if two elements are equal: if arr[i] == arr[j], then they might be duplicates. Time complexity for basic access is O(1), and space complexity is O(n) for storing the array. Connection to L4: Arrays are the structure we iterate over. Connection to higher levels: This foundation supports all operations in the hash set approach. Common mistakes: confusing arrays with lists (in some languages), not understanding zero-based indexing, or mishandling integer bounds. Real code pattern: In Python, arrays are often lists: nums = [1,2,3]; to access: nums[0] gives 1; to compare: nums[0] == nums[1] is False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has elements at indices 0,1,2. Step 2: Access index 1: nums[1] = 10. Step 3: Return 10.
Step 1: Access nums[0] = 3. Step 2: Access nums[2] = 9. Step 3: Compare 3 == 9: False.

approaches.name: Sort and Scan O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then scan adjacent elements to find the duplicate. This reduces time complexity compared to brute force but requires sorting.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) depending on sorting implementation

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Duplicate Number Using Sort and Scan
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort array then scan adjacent elements to find duplicate.
ladders.explanation:
This approach solves the duplicate number problem by first sorting the array to bring identical elements together, then scanning through the sorted array to find where two consecutive elements match. The intuition is that sorting transforms an unordered search problem into a simple linear scan problem. After sorting, duplicates become adjacent neighbors, making detection trivial. The algorithm works because sorting guarantees that identical values will be consecutive in the sorted order. The time complexity is dominated by the sorting step at O(n log n), while space complexity depends on whether we sort in-place (O(1)) or create a new sorted array (O(n)). The key insight is trading time efficiency for algorithmic simplicity—while not optimal for this specific problem, it demonstrates how sorting can simplify pattern recognition. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle edge cases like arrays with no duplicates (though constraints guarantee one), or incorrectly assuming the duplicate appears only once (it could appear multiple times). The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the sorting subroutine with the scanning logic.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Sort the input array (using any stable sort algorithm)
2. Initialize index i = 0
3. While i < length(array) - 1:
   a. If array[i] == array[i+1]: return array[i]
   b. Increment i
4. Return -1 (though problem guarantees duplicate exists)

Time: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for scanning = O(n log n)
Space: O(1) if in-place sort, O(n) if not

Real code pattern:
def findDuplicate(nums):
    nums.sort()  # In-place sort, O(n log n)
    for i in range(len(nums)-1):
        if nums[i] == nums[i+1]:
            return nums[i]
    return -1

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort array → [1,2,3,3,4]
Step 2: i=0, compare nums[0]=1 vs nums[1]=2 → different
Step 3: i=1, compare nums[1]=2 vs nums[2]=3 → different
Step 4: i=2, compare nums[2]=3 vs nums[3]=3 → EQUAL, return 3

Step 1: Sort array → [1,2,2,3,4]
Step 2: i=0, compare nums[0]=1 vs nums[1]=2 → different
Step 3: i=1, compare nums[1]=2 vs nums[2]=2 → EQUAL, return 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Sorting the Array
ladders.desc: Implement sorting algorithm to arrange elements in ascending order.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the sorting component of the duplicate finding approach. The intuition is that by arranging elements in sorted order, we transform the problem from searching for duplicates in an unordered collection to simply checking adjacent elements. Any comparison-based sort will work, but for simplicity we'll use a standard library sort (like Timsort in Python) which provides O(n log n) average performance. The key insight is that sorting creates structure from chaos—identical values become neighbors. This subroutine is critical because without proper sorting, the subsequent scan wouldn't work efficiently. Time complexity is O(n log n) for comparison sorts, space complexity is O(1) for in-place sorts or O(n) for merge-style sorts. Common mistakes include using unstable sorts that might not preserve relative order (though not critical here), or implementing inefficient O(n²) sorts for large n. The connection to L0 is that this sorted array becomes the input for the duplicate scanning logic.

Step-by-step algorithm (using quicksort as example):
1. If array length ≤ 1, return array
2. Choose pivot element
3. Partition array into elements < pivot, = pivot, > pivot
4. Recursively sort left and right partitions
5. Return concatenated result

Time: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst-case for quicksort
Space: O(log n) for recursion stack

Real code pattern:
def sortArray(nums):
    # Using built-in sort for simplicity
    nums.sort()
    return nums
# Or implement custom sort like:
def quicksort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    pivot = arr[len(arr)//2]
    left = [x for x in arr if x < pivot]
    middle = [x for x in arr if x == pivot]
    right = [x for x in arr if x > pivot]
    return quicksort(left) + middle + quicksort(right)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Input [3,1,4,2]
Step 2: Choose pivot = 4 (middle element)
Step 3: Partition: left=[3,1,2], middle=[4], right=[]
Step 4: Recursively sort left: pivot=1 → left=[], middle=[1], right=[3,2]
Step 5: Sort right of left: [3,2] → pivot=2 → left=[], middle=[2], right=[3]
Step 6: Combine: []+[1]+[2]+[3] = [1,2,3]
Step 7: Combine with original: [1,2,3]+[4]+[] = [1,2,3,4]

Step 1: Input [5,2,5,1,3]
Step 2: Choose pivot = 5 (middle element)
Step 3: Partition: left=[2,1,3], middle=[5,5], right=[]
Step 4: Recursively sort left: [2,1,3] → pivot=1 → left=[], middle=[1], right=[2,3]
Step 5: Sort right of left: [2,3] → pivot=3 → left=[2], middle=[3], right=[]
Step 6: Sort left of right: [2] → returns [2]
Step 7: Combine left parts: []+[1]+[2]+[3] = [1,2,3]
Step 8: Final combine: [1,2,3]+[5,5]+[] = [1,2,3,5,5]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Scanning Adjacent Elements in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: After sorting, scan through array comparing consecutive elements.
ladders.explanation:
This is the core detection logic that finds the duplicate once the array is sorted. The intuition is beautifully simple: in a sorted array, identical values must be adjacent. Therefore, we only need to check each element against its immediate neighbor. This reduces what could be an O(n²) pairwise comparison problem to a simple O(n) linear scan. The algorithm works by iterating through the array from index 0 to n-1 (where n is array length), comparing element i with element i+1. If they match, we've found our duplicate. The key insight is leveraging the sorted property to eliminate most comparisons. Time complexity is O(n) for the scan, space is O(1). Common mistakes include going out of bounds (comparing last element with non-existent next), or stopping after first match when there might be multiple duplicates (though problem guarantees exactly one duplicate). The connection to L1 is that this logic takes the sorted array as input and produces the duplicate as output.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: sorted array arr of length n
2. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. If arr[i] == arr[i+1]: return arr[i]
3. Return -1 (though shouldn't happen with valid input)

Time: O(n) for linear scan
Space: O(1) for index variables

Real code pattern:
def findDuplicateInSorted(arr):
    for i in range(len(arr)-1):
        if arr[i] == arr[i+1]:
            return arr[i]
    return -1

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2 → different
Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3 → different
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=3, arr[3]=3 → EQUAL, return 3
Step 1: i=0, arr[0]=1, arr[1]=1 → EQUAL, return 1

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Pairwise Comparison
ladders.desc: Iterate through array comparing each element with the next one.
ladders.explanation:
This intermediate concept teaches how to traverse an array while comparing adjacent elements—a fundamental pattern in many algorithms. The intuition is that many array problems require examining relationships between consecutive elements. By maintaining an index and accessing arr[i] and arr[i+1], we can detect patterns like duplicates, increasing sequences, or local maxima. This building block is essential because it demonstrates how to safely navigate array boundaries while performing comparisons. The algorithm must handle the edge case of the last element having no next neighbor. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting each element once, space is O(1). Common mistakes include index out-of-range errors (trying to access arr[i+1] when i is the last index), or off-by-one errors in loop boundaries. The connection to L2 is that this general array traversal technique becomes specialized for duplicate detection when applied to a sorted array.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: array arr of length n
2. For i from 0 to n-2:
   a. Perform comparison operation on arr[i] and arr[i+1]
   b. Take action based on comparison result
3. Process results

Time: O(n)
Space: O(1)

Real code pattern:
def traverseAndCompare(arr):
    results = []
    for i in range(len(arr)-1):  # Stop before last element
        # Compare arr[i] and arr[i+1]
        if arr[i] == arr[i+1]:
            results.append((i, 'equal'))
        elif arr[i] < arr[i+1]:
            results.append((i, 'increasing'))
        else:
            results.append((i, 'decreasing'))
    return results

examples.trace:
Step 1: i=0, compare arr[0]=1 vs arr[1]=2 → 1<2 → 'increasing'
Step 2: i=1, compare arr[1]=2 vs arr[2]=3 → 2<3 → 'increasing'
Step 3: i=2, compare arr[2]=3 vs arr[3]=4 → 3<4 → 'increasing'
Step 4: i=3, compare arr[3]=4 vs arr[4]=5 → 4<5 → 'increasing'

Step 1: i=0, compare arr[0]=5 vs arr[1]=4 → 5>4 → 'decreasing'
Step 2: i=1, compare arr[1]=4 vs arr[2]=3 → 4>3 → 'decreasing'
Step 3: i=2, compare arr[2]=3 vs arr[3]=2 → 3>2 → 'decreasing'
Step 4: i=3, compare arr[3]=2 vs arr[4]=1 → 2>1 → 'decreasing'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Equality Check
ladders.desc: Compare two values and determine if they are equal.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation is the atomic unit of the duplicate detection algorithm: comparing two values for equality. The intuition is that duplicate detection ultimately reduces to answering the question 'are these two values the same?' This operation seems trivial but is conceptually profound—it's the basis of pattern recognition in arrays. The comparison operation works by examining the binary representation or value of two elements and returning true if they are identical. In programming languages, this is typically implemented with the '==' operator. Time complexity is O(1) for primitive types, space is O(1). Common mistakes include confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), or comparing objects by reference instead of value. The connection to L3 is that this basic comparison operation is what gets repeated during array traversal to detect duplicates.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Input: two values a and b
2. Compare a and b using equality operator
3. Return true if equal, false otherwise

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

Real code pattern:
def areEqual(a, b):
    return a == b
# Usage examples:
# areEqual(5, 5) → True
# areEqual(3, 7) → False
# areEqual('hello', 'hello') → True

examples.trace:
Step 1: Receive inputs a=5, b=5
Step 2: Compare values: 5 == 5
Step 3: Values are identical
Step 4: Return True

Step 1: Receive inputs a=3, b=7
Step 2: Compare values: 3 == 7
Step 3: Values are different (3 ≠ 7)
Step 4: Return False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Fundamental concept: arrays as ordered collections accessible by index.
ladders.explanation:
This is the absolute foundation: understanding what an array is and how to access its elements. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via a numerical index starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays provide random access—we can instantly access any element if we know its position. This is crucial for the duplicate finding algorithm because we need to access consecutive elements (arr[i] and arr[i+1]) efficiently. Arrays work by allocating a block of memory where each element sits at a predictable offset from the start. Time complexity for access is O(1), space is O(n) for n elements. Common beginner mistakes include using 1-based indexing (thinking first element is at index 1), accessing out-of-bounds indices, or confusing array length with last index. The connection to L4 is that once we understand array indexing, we can retrieve two elements to compare them for equality.

Key concepts:
1. Array declaration/initialization
2. Indexing (arr[0] is first element)
3. Length property (number of elements)
4. Iteration basics

Real code pattern:
# Creating an array
arr = [3, 1, 4, 2]

# Accessing elements
first = arr[0]  # Gets 3
second = arr[1] # Gets 1

# Getting length
n = len(arr)    # Gets 4

# Last element is at index n-1
last = arr[n-1] # Gets 2

# Iterating
for i in range(len(arr)):
    element = arr[i]

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array has elements [10, 20, 30, 40]
Step 2: Indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30, 3→40
Step 3: Request index 2
Step 4: Index 2 corresponds to third element (remember 0-based)
Step 5: Return value 30

Step 1: Array has 3 elements
Step 2: Index 0: arr[0] = 5
Step 3: Index 1: arr[1] = 8
Step 4: Index 2: arr[2] = 2
Step 5: All elements: 5, 8, 2

approaches.name: Floyd's Tortoise and Hare O(n)
approaches.summary: Treat the array as a linked list with cycles, using Floyd's cycle detection algorithm to find the duplicate without extra space. This is optimal for time and space.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Duplicate Number Using Floyd's Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm to find the duplicate number in an array of n+1 integers between 1 and n.
ladders.explanation:
This problem can be solved optimally using Floyd's cycle detection algorithm by treating the array as a linked list where each element points to the index equal to its value. Since there's a duplicate number, this creates a cycle in the linked list representation. The algorithm works in two phases: first, detect the cycle using slow and fast pointers (tortoise and hare), then find the entrance to the cycle which corresponds to the duplicate number.

Intuition: Each element in the array can be seen as a node that points to another node (the value at that index). Because numbers are between 1 and n and there are n+1 elements, by the pigeonhole principle, at least one number is repeated. This repetition creates a cycle when we follow the pointers. Floyd's algorithm efficiently finds where this cycle begins without needing extra space.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize two pointers: slow = nums[0], fast = nums[0]
2. Phase 1 (Cycle Detection):
   - Move slow one step: slow = nums[slow]
   - Move fast two steps: fast = nums[nums[fast]]
   - Repeat until slow == fast (they meet inside the cycle)
3. Phase 2 (Find Cycle Entrance):
   - Reset slow to nums[0]
   - Move both pointers one step at a time: slow = nums[slow], fast = nums[fast]
   - When they meet again, that's the duplicate number

Time Complexity: O(n) - Each pointer traverses at most n steps
Space Complexity: O(1) - Only two pointers used

Connection to L1: This full solution combines the cycle detection (L1) with the cycle entrance finding logic.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting both pointers at index 0 instead of nums[0]
2. Not handling the reset properly in phase 2
3. Confusing array indices with array values

Real code pattern:
def findDuplicate(nums):
    # Phase 1: Find intersection point
    slow = nums[0]
    fast = nums[0]
    while True:
        slow = nums[slow]
        fast = nums[nums[fast]]
        if slow == fast:
            break
    
    # Phase 2: Find entrance to cycle
    slow = nums[0]
    while slow != fast:
        slow = nums[slow]
        fast = nums[fast]
    
    return slow

examples.trace:
Array: [1,3,4,2,2]
Phase 1 (Cycle Detection):
Initialize: slow = nums[0] = 1, fast = nums[0] = 1
Step 1: slow = nums[1] = 3, fast = nums[nums[1]] = nums[3] = 2
Step 2: slow = nums[3] = 2, fast = nums[nums[2]] = nums[4] = 2
Now slow = 2, fast = 2 → they meet

Phase 2 (Find Entrance):
Reset slow = nums[0] = 1
Step 1: slow = nums[1] = 3, fast = nums[2] = 4
Step 2: slow = nums[3] = 2, fast = nums[4] = 2
Now slow = 2, fast = 2 → duplicate found = 2

Array: [3,1,3,4,2]
Phase 1 (Cycle Detection):
Initialize: slow = nums[0] = 3, fast = nums[0] = 3
Step 1: slow = nums[3] = 4, fast = nums[nums[3]] = nums[4] = 2
Step 2: slow = nums[4] = 2, fast = nums[nums[2]] = nums[3] = 4
Step 3: slow = nums[2] = 3, fast = nums[nums[4]] = nums[2] = 3
Now slow = 3, fast = 3 → they meet

Phase 2 (Find Entrance):
Reset slow = nums[0] = 3
Step 1: slow = nums[3] = 4, fast = nums[3] = 4
Now slow = 4, fast = 4 → but wait, we need to continue until they meet at different positions
Actually: slow = 3, fast = 3 already match → duplicate found = 3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Floyd's Cycle Detection in Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Implement Floyd's algorithm to detect a cycle in a linked list using slow and fast pointers.
ladders.explanation:
Floyd's cycle detection algorithm, also known as the tortoise and hare algorithm, is a fundamental technique for detecting cycles in linked lists without using extra memory. The intuition is simple: if you have two pointers moving at different speeds through a linked list, and there's a cycle, the faster pointer will eventually lap the slower pointer inside the cycle.

Why it works: In a cyclic structure, the fast pointer (moving 2 steps) gains 1 step on the slow pointer (moving 1 step) with each iteration. Eventually, the distance between them becomes a multiple of the cycle length, causing them to meet. If there's no cycle, the fast pointer will reach the end (null) first.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize slow = head, fast = head
2. While fast != null and fast.next != null:
   - Move slow one step: slow = slow.next
   - Move fast two steps: fast = fast.next.next
   - If slow == fast: return true (cycle detected)
3. Return false (no cycle)

Time Complexity: O(n) - Each pointer visits each node at most once
Space Complexity: O(1) - Only two pointers used

Connection to L0: This cycle detection is the first phase of the full duplicate finding algorithm.

Common mistakes:
1. Not checking both fast != null and fast.next != null before moving
2. Starting with incorrect initial positions
3. Moving pointers in wrong order

Real code pattern:
def hasCycle(head):
    if not head:
        return False
    
    slow = head
    fast = head
    
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        
        if slow == fast:
            return True
    
    return False

examples.trace:
List representation: 1→3→4→2→2 (with last node pointing to second node)
Initialize: slow = 1, fast = 1
Step 1: slow = 3, fast = 4 (fast moved two steps: 1→3→4)
Step 2: slow = 4, fast = 2 (fast: 4→2→2)
Step 3: slow = 2, fast = 4 (fast: 2→2→4)
Step 4: slow = 2, fast = 2 (fast: 4→2→2)
Now slow = 2, fast = 2 → cycle detected

List: 1→2→3→4→5→null
Initialize: slow = 1, fast = 1
Step 1: slow = 2, fast = 3
Step 2: slow = 3, fast = 5
Step 3: slow = 4, fast = null (can't move two steps from 5)
Loop ends, fast reached null → no cycle

ladders.title: Core Logic — Array as Linked List with Cycle Detection
ladders.desc: Transform array indexing into linked list traversal to detect cycles caused by duplicates.
ladders.explanation:
The key insight for solving the duplicate number problem is recognizing that an array can be interpreted as a linked list where each element points to another element. Given an array nums of length n+1 with values between 1 and n, we can treat each index i as a node that points to node nums[i]. This creates a linked list structure without explicitly building nodes.

Intuition: Since values are between 1 and n, they are valid indices in the array (0-based indexing requires adjustment). The duplicate value creates a situation where two different indices point to the same next node, forming a cycle. Floyd's algorithm can then detect this cycle.

Why this works: Each number in the array serves as both a value and a pointer to the next position. The constraint that numbers are between 1 and n ensures all pointers stay within array bounds (after adjusting for 0-based indexing). The duplicate means some value appears twice, so two different nodes point to the same next node, creating a cycle.

Step-by-step:
1. Start at index 0 (or nums[0] for 1-based values)
2. Move to next node: current = nums[current]
3. Repeat until cycle detected or end reached

Time Complexity: O(n) for traversal
Space Complexity: O(1) for pointers

Connection to L1: This is the specific application of cycle detection to array-based linked lists.

Common mistakes:
1. Not adjusting for 0-based vs 1-based indexing
2. Confusing array values with array indices
3. Not understanding why duplicate creates cycle

Real code pattern:
def traverseAsLinkedList(nums):
    visited = set()
    current = 0
    
    while current < len(nums):
        if current in visited:
            return True  # Cycle detected
        visited.add(current)
        current = nums[current]  # Move to next node
    
    return False

examples.trace:
Array as linked list: 
Index 0 (value 1) → Index 1 (value 3) → Index 3 (value 2) → Index 2 (value 4) → Index 4 (value 2) → Index 2 (value 4) ...
Traversal: 
Start at index 0: value 1
Move to index 1: value 3
Move to index 3: value 2
Move to index 2: value 4
Move to index 4: value 2
Move to index 2: value 4 (repeated!) → Cycle detected
This happens because both index 3 and index 4 point to index 2

Array as linked list:
Index 0 (value 1) → Index 1 (value 2) → Index 2 (value 3) → Index 3 (value 4) → Index 4 (value 5) → Would go to index 5 which is out of bounds
Traversal:
Start at index 0: value 1
Move to index 1: value 2
Move to index 2: value 3
Move to index 3: value 4
Move to index 4: value 5
Next would be index 5 (out of bounds) → No cycle

ladders.title: Building Block — Pointer Movement in Array-Based Structures
ladders.desc: Practice moving pointers through arrays using values as next indices.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental skill involves traversing an array where each element contains the index of the next element to visit. This is the core operation needed for treating arrays as linked lists. The key insight is that array values can serve as pointers to other array positions.

Intuition: In many algorithms, we need to follow a path through an array where the next position is determined by the current value. This creates a chain of references that can be followed until we reach a terminal condition (like a repeated position or out-of-bounds).

Why this is important: This pattern appears in various algorithms including cycle detection, graph traversal on implicit graphs, and permutation problems. Mastering this allows you to work with implicit data structures without building explicit nodes.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Start at initial position (often 0 or given start)
2. While termination condition not met:
   - Read current value
   - Use value as next index (may need index adjustment)
   - Update current position
   - Check for termination (repeated position, specific value, out-of-bounds)

Time Complexity: O(n) in worst case
Space Complexity: O(1) for simple traversal

Connection to L2: This is the mechanical skill needed to implement the array-as-linked-list concept.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling 0-based vs 1-based indexing correctly
2. Infinite loops when cycle exists
3. Not checking array bounds

Real code pattern:
def traverseWithPointers(nums, start):
    current = start
    steps = 0
    max_steps = len(nums)
    
    while steps < max_steps:
        print(f"At index {current}, value {nums[current]}")
        current = nums[current]  # Move to next position
        steps += 1
        
        if current < 0 or current >= len(nums):
            break
    
    return steps

examples.trace:
Start at index 0: value = 2
Move to index 2: value = 1
Move to index 1: value = 3
Move to index 3: value = 4
Move to index 4: out of bounds → stop
Path: 0→2→1→3→(4 out of bounds)
Total steps: 4

Start at index 0: value = 1
Move to index 1: value = 2
Move to index 2: value = 3
Move to index 3: value = 0
Move to index 0: value = 1 (back to start)
This creates a cycle: 0→1→2→3→0→1→...
Without termination check, this would loop forever

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Value Access
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of reading array values and using them as indices.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most basic building block: understanding how to access array elements and use their values to determine subsequent array positions. In programming, arrays are contiguous blocks of memory where each element can be accessed in constant time using its index.

Intuition: An array is like a numbered list of boxes. Each box (element) has a position number (index) and contains a value. We can use the value in one box to tell us which other box to look at next. This creates chains of references through the array.

Why this matters: This pattern is fundamental to many algorithms. The ability to follow references through an array is crucial for understanding more complex concepts like linked lists, graphs, and state machines implemented as arrays.

Key operations:
1. Access element at index i: value = array[i]
2. Use value as new index: next_index = value
3. Check if index is valid: 0 <= index < length

Time Complexity: O(1) for each access
Space Complexity: O(1) for the operation itself

Connection to L3: This is the atomic operation that makes pointer movement possible.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors with array indices
2. Not checking array bounds before access
3. Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing

Real code pattern:
def followChain(nums, start, steps):
    current = start
    path = []
    
    for i in range(steps):
        if current < 0 or current >= len(nums):
            break
        path.append(current)
        current = nums[current]  # Use value as next index
    
    return path

examples.trace:
Step 1: At index 0, value = 3 → next index = 3
Step 2: At index 3, value = 2 → next index = 2
Step 3: At index 2, value = 4 → next index = 4
Path recorded: [0, 3, 2]
Note: We don't include the final index 4 since we only record starting positions

Step 1: Start at index 2, value = 0 → next index = 0
Step 2: At index 0, value = 1 → next index = 1
Step 3: At index 1, value = 2 → next index = 2
Step 4: At index 2, value = 0 → next index = 0
Path: [2, 0, 1, 2]
This shows a cycle: 2→0→1→2→0→...

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding arrays, indices, and basic traversal for complete beginners.
ladders.explanation:
An array is a fundamental data structure that stores a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element has an index (position number) starting from 0. Arrays allow random access - you can directly access any element if you know its index.

Intuition: Think of an array as a row of numbered lockers. Each locker has a number (index) and contains something (value). You can open any locker instantly if you know its number. The values inside lockers can be anything - numbers, text, or even instructions telling you which other locker to open next.

Why this is foundational: Arrays are building blocks for almost all data structures and algorithms. Understanding how to create, access, and traverse arrays is essential before learning more complex concepts.

Basic operations:
1. Declaration: arr = [value1, value2, value3]
2. Access: value = arr[index]
3. Update: arr[index] = new_value
4. Length: n = len(arr)
5. Traversal: for i in range(len(arr)): process arr[i]

Time Complexity: O(1) for access, O(n) for traversal
Space Complexity: O(n) to store n elements

Connection to L4: This basic understanding enables the value-as-index pattern.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Thinking indices start at 1 (they start at 0)
2. Accessing indices outside array bounds
3. Confusing the index with the value at that index

Real code pattern:
def arrayBasics():
    # Create array
    arr = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
    
    # Access elements
    print("Element at index 0:", arr[0])  # 10
    print("Element at index 2:", arr[2])  # 30
    
    # Update element
    arr[1] = 25
    print("After update:", arr)  # [10, 25, 30, 40, 50]
    
    # Traverse array
    print("Traversal:")
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        print(f"Index {i}: Value {arr[i]}")

examples.trace:
Array: [5, 10, 15, 20]
Length: 4 elements
Indices: 0, 1, 2, 3

Access examples:
arr[0] = 5
arr[1] = 10
arr[2] = 15
arr[3] = 20

Traversal:
Index 0 → Value 5
Index 1 → Value 10
Index 2 → Value 15
Index 3 → Value 20

Update example:
Set arr[1] = 12
New array: [5, 12, 15, 20]

Array: [100, 200, 300]

Important distinction:
The INDEX is the position: 0, 1, 2
The VALUE is what's stored: 100, 200, 300

Common confusion:
"The second element" = index 1, value 200
"The element at position 2" = index 2, value 300

Access using values as indices:
If we try arr[100] → ERROR! Index 100 doesn't exist
If we try arr[arr[0]] = arr[100] → ERROR!
But arr[arr[0] // 100] = arr[1] = 200 ✓

testCases.input: Array: [5, 15, 25], Set arr[1] = 20, What is arr[1] now?
Array: [8, 6, 4, 2], What is the last index?
